name: ddd-context-mapping
description: Map relationships between bounded contexts and define integration contracts using DDD context mapping patterns.
capabilities: Identify and map relationships between bounded contexts in your domain
Select appropriate context mapping patterns like Partnership, Customer-Supplier, or Conformist
Define anti-corruption layer boundaries to protect domain integrity
Create contract ownership matrices for clear team responsibilities
Document translation rules between different ubiquitous languages
Establish failure modes and versioning policies for context integrations
author: sickn33
securitySummary: Static analysis flagged markdown backticks as shell commands and weak cryptography patterns. All findings are FALSE POSITIVES - the skill contains only documentation and reference material with no executable code, network calls, or filesystem operations. Safe for publication.
valueStatement: Domain-Driven Design integration becomes complex when multiple bounded contexts interact. This skill defines clear contracts and anti-corruption layers between contexts using proven DDD patterns.
userTitle: Map bounded context relationships with DDD patterns
limitations: Does not replace detailed API schema design or technical contract specifications
Cannot guarantee organizational alignment without team buy-in and process changes
Requires periodic review when team ownership or business domains evolve
Focuses on strategic DDD patterns, not tactical implementation details

useCases.title: Microservices Integration Planning
useCases.description: Map how Checkout, Billing, Inventory, and Fraud contexts integrate before implementing service boundaries.

useCases.targetUser: Engineering leads managing legacy modernization projects
useCases.title: Legacy System Migration
useCases.description: Define anti-corruption layers when integrating new domains with existing monolithic systems.

useCases.targetUser: Technical product managers coordinating multi-team initiatives
useCases.title: Cross-Team Contract Definition
useCases.description: Clarify upstream and downstream ownership to prevent domain leakage and unclear responsibilities.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Context Mapping
promptTemplates.prompt: Analyze my domain with these bounded contexts: [list contexts]. Identify the relationships between each pair and recommend appropriate context mapping patterns from DDD.
promptTemplates.scenario: Initial domain analysis for a new system

promptTemplates.title: Anti-Corruption Layer Design
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to integrate with [external system/context]. Design an anti-corruption layer that translates their model into my ubiquitous language while protecting my domain core.
promptTemplates.scenario: Third-party integration with different domain model

promptTemplates.title: Contract Ownership Matrix
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a contract ownership matrix for these context pairs: [list pairs]. Define who owns each contract, what translation is needed, and the coupling risk level.

promptTemplates.title: Failure Mode Analysis
promptTemplates.prompt: For the [upstream] to [downstream] context integration using [pattern], identify failure modes, define fallback behaviors, and establish a versioning policy.

bestPractices: Keep anti-corruption layer code at domain boundaries, not inside domain core
Add contract tests to verify translated behavior matches expected semantics
Revisit context maps when team ownership or business domains change
antiPatterns: Allowing downstream contexts to depend directly on upstream internal models
Creating shared kernels between contexts that should remain independent
Skipping translation layers when integrating with external or legacy systems

faq.question: When should I use an Anti-Corruption Layer versus a Conformist pattern?
faq.answer: Use ACL when the upstream model conflicts with your ubiquitous language or when integrating with external systems. Use Conformist when the upstream model is stable and aligns with your needs.

faq.question: How many bounded contexts should a typical system have?
faq.answer: There is no fixed number. Contexts emerge from business domains and team boundaries. Start with major business capabilities and split when you detect conflicting ubiquitous languages.

faq.question: Can a context have multiple relationship patterns with another context?
faq.answer: Yes, different aggregates within contexts may use different patterns. Document each relationship separately to avoid confusion.

faq.question: What is a Shared Kernel and when should I avoid it?
faq.answer: Shared Kernel is a subset of the domain model shared by two teams. Avoid it when contexts need independent evolution or when team coordination overhead becomes too high.

faq.question: How do I handle versioning when context contracts change?
faq.answer: Establish a versioning policy upfront. Support backward compatibility during transition periods. Use contract tests to detect breaking changes before deployment.

faq.question: Does this skill work for monolithic applications?
faq.answer: Yes, bounded contexts can exist within monoliths as modular boundaries. Context mapping helps plan future service extraction and prevents domain leakage across modules.
